name: Mishipeshu (Underwater Panther)
shortDescription: Mishipeshu is a powerful and dangerous underwater panther from Algonquian mythology. It is often depicted as a composite creature with feline and reptilian features, associated with water and copper.
description: Mishipeshu, also known as the Underwater Panther or Great Lynx, is a prominent figure in the mythology of many Algonquian-speaking Native American tribes, particularly those around the Great Lakes. It is a powerful and often malevolent spirit, associated with water, copper, and unpredictable forces. Depictions vary, but Mishipeshu is commonly portrayed as a feline creature with scales, horns, and sometimes copper spines. Its body is often described as resembling a panther or lynx, but with features borrowed from reptiles, fish, or other aquatic animals. It is believed to inhabit deep lakes, rivers, and other bodies of water, and is capable of causing storms, floods, and other natural disasters. Mishipeshu is considered a guardian of copper, a valuable resource in the region, and its presence near copper deposits is both a sign of abundance and a warning of danger. Offerings are sometimes made to appease Mishipeshu and ensure safe passage on the water. In some traditions, it is viewed as an enemy of the Thunderbird, a powerful spirit of the sky and storms, representing the constant conflict between water and sky, chaos and order.
appearance: Feline body (panther or lynx), scales, horns, copper spines, reptilian or fish-like features.

sources.title: The Mishipeshu: Mythical Water Panther of the Great Lakes
sources.author: Carolyn Podruchny

sources.title: Sacred Stories of the Sweet Grass Cree
sources.author: Leonard Bloomfield
